comments:
Summary:
This manuscript investigates the use of a multilayer perceptron (deep learning) to classify gas–liquid two-phase flow patterns using a published experimental database (Shoham). Three classification setups are considered (6-class, 5-class with stratified merged, and 3-class aggregated) and reported test accuracies are ~83.9%, 83.3% and 86.0% respectively. The authors use the H2O library and mention regularization and ReLU activations.

Major strengths:
- Application is relevant to the oil & gas / multiphase flow community and uses a relatively large, public experimental dataset (5676 points) spanning a wide range of inclinations and diameters.
- Showing that a standard MLP can achieve reasonably high accuracy for several class groupings is a useful preliminary result and may motivate further study.

Major weaknesses / concerns:
1) Lack of methodological detail: the paper repeatedly references “six types of input features”, model architecture and hyperparameters (Table 1), regularization strengths, number of neurons/layers, training procedure, and exact nfolds settings but does not provide these details in the text available here. This prevents reproducibility and assessment of whether the model was appropriately tuned.
2) Evaluation is limited: only overall accuracy on a single random split is reported. No per-class precision/recall, F1, confusion-matrix numbers (only qualitative remarks), class imbalance statistics, or confidence intervals/statistical significance are given. For multi-class problems with imbalanced classes, accuracy alone is insufficient.
3) Comparison to prior work is weak: the paper claims to “outperform results from previous studies” but provides no quantitative baseline numbers or references to comparable performance metrics. Without direct comparison (same data splits or cross-validation) the claim is unsupported.
4) No ablation or robustness analysis: there is no assessment of which input features matter most, how sensitive results are to architecture/hyperparameters, or whether simpler classifiers (logistic regression, random forests, SVM) would achieve similar performance. This is important to justify the use of deep models over simpler approaches.
5) Dataset labeling issues are acknowledged but not handled: the authors note subjective experimental labeling differences as a source of misclassification, but do not attempt to quantify inter-source label consistency or adjust evaluation accordingly.
6) Presentation and formatting problems: the manuscript contains duplicated paragraphs, messy reference markers, and missing table contents in the provided text. This reduces clarity.

Recommendations to improve the paper:
- Provide full model details (feature list and preprocessing, network architecture, hyperparameters, regularization values, training epochs, optimizer settings, and exact cross-validation procedure) and make code/weights available.
- Report class distributions and per-class metrics (precision/recall/F1), and include confusion matrices for test sets. Provide confidence intervals by repeated runs or cross-validation.
- Include comparisons to baseline non-deep methods (e.g., random forests, SVM, logistic regression) and to prior published results on the same dataset, using comparable evaluation protocols.
- Perform ablation studies to show which input features contribute most and how sensitive results are to architecture choices.
- Address label noise/ambiguity explicitly (e.g., by grouping borderline classes, using probabilistic labels, or assessing inter-source agreement).
- Clean up presentation, fix duplicated text and references, and ensure tables/figures are readable.

Overall assessment:
The paper presents a reasonable application of standard deep learning tools to an engineering dataset and reports encouraging accuracy numbers. However, the manuscript lacks sufficient methodological and experimental rigor and comparative analysis to substantiate its main claims, limiting its scientific contribution in the current form. With the suggested additions and clearer presentation this work could be a useful contribution to the community.
